itineraries.title: Complete Polynesia: Society & Tuamotu Islands in 20 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Papeete to the atolls of Rangiroa and Fakarava, a journey between volcanoes, lagoons and shark passes across two archipelagos
itineraries.notes: 20-day circuit covering the Society Islands (Tahiti, Moorea, Huahine, Raiatea, Taha'a, Bora Bora) and the Tuamotus (Rangiroa, Fakarava). Inter-island flights and ferry. Budget for 5-6 domestic flights.

visits.text: Papeete's market, Mapuru a Paraita, occupies a two-story covered building right in the city center. Vanilla pods, handmade monoï oil, fresh tiare flower crowns, and colorful tifaifai quilts pile high on the stalls. Sunday mornings from 4 a.m. onward, Tahitian families flood the aisles—it's the most authentic time to visit.

Step inside Notre-Dame Cathedral, recognizable by its bright yellow facade and bell tower dominating downtown. Built starting in 1856 and completed in 1875, it houses stained glass windows that filter the tropical light and offer welcome coolness.
Head to Paofai Gardens along the waterfront. This planted promenade facing the harbor offers an unobstructed view of Moorea's silhouette. Outrigger canoes (va'a) train here in late afternoon.
The Pearl Museum traces the history of pearl farming in French Polynesia. In 45 minutes, you'll learn to distinguish the luster, orient, and shapes of Tahitian black pearls.
Walk up Boulevard Pomaré to spot the street art murals created during the Ono'u festival. International artists have transformed several downtown facades into an open-air gallery.

activities.text: The roulottes at Place Vaiete set up every evening from 6 p.m. facing the harbor. Poisson cru with coconut milk, chao mein, grilled shrimp—a local institution under the stars.

practical_info.tip: Arrive early for the best table selection

activities.text: If you arrive on a Sunday, head to Paofai Protestant Temple to hear the polyphonic religious singing. Worshippers wear hats and white outfits—a unique sonic and visual experience.

map_keywords: Museum of Tahiti and Islands Punaauia

visits.text: The three Faarumai waterfalls are at PK 22 on the east coast, 22 km from Papeete. The first, Vaimahuta, plunges 80 meters down a basalt wall—five minutes' walk from the parking area. The other two, Haamaremare Rahi and Iti, require an additional 20 minutes on a steeper trail.

Stop at the Arahoho Blowhole, just beside the waterfalls. The ocean rushes into a lava tube carved by erosion beneath the road and shoots a powerful jet of water landward. In Tahitian, Arahoho means 'the road that roars.' The spectacle depends on the swell—the stronger it is, the more impressive the spray.
Take the coastal road to Pointe Vénus in Mahina (PK 10). The white 25-meter lighthouse, built in 1867, marks where Europeans landed in 1767. Black sand beach and views of Moorea at sunset.
The Tahara'a lookout, at the start of the east coast leaving Papeete, offers from its landscaped terrace a panorama over the lagoon, the coral barrier, and the Tahiti Iti peninsula.

The Museum of Tahiti and Her Islands in Punaauia on the west coast (PK 15) traces the natural and cultural history of Polynesia. Collections of tapa cloth, traditional canoes, and basalt tools. Check opening days before visiting.

activities.text: Swimming at a black sand beach along the east coast. The water is warm year-round but currents can be strong—respect the flags and stick to supervised areas.
Vaiava Beach (PK 18, Punaauia) offers white sand on the west side and easy snorkeling above coral heads, with direct views of Moorea.

visits.text: The ferry crosses the Vaiare pass and reveals Moorea, Tahiti's sister island. From the dock, the trident-shaped silhouette of its mountains dominates: Cook's Bay on the left, Opunohu Bay on the right, separated by the sacred Mount Rotui (899 m).

Climb the shaded little road to Opunohu Lookout, at 240 meters altitude in the heart of a 9-km-diameter crater. The 360° panorama embraces the two bays, eight mountain ranges, the lagoon, and pineapple fields below.
On the way down, stop at the Opunohu Agricultural School. Students produce tropical fruit jams and valley pineapple juice. Archaeological sites (marae) are nearby, along the marked trail.
Marae Titiroa, a Polynesian stone temple nestled in the vegetation of Opunohu Valley, testifies to the ancient Ma'ohi religion. Explanatory panels line the site.
Ta'ahiamanu public beach, in Opunohu Bay, unfurls a strip of white sand bordered by coconut palms. The shallow, calm water makes it a family-friendly swimming spot.

activities.text: Snorkeling in the lagoon on the Cook's Bay side: butterflyfish, surgeonfish, and small rays glide above corals accessible from the beach. Mornings offer optimal visibility.

practical_info.tip: Mask and snorkel are enough—no need for fins

activities.text: Lagoon boat tour between the two bays. You cruise at the foot of Mount Rotui, encountering stingrays and small blacktip reef sharks in the shallow waters around the motus.

visits.text: Te Fare Natura, Moorea's eco-museum, is located at the mouth of Opunohu Bay. Interactive exhibits on reef ecosystems, endemic fauna, and the island's volcanic geology.
Detour to the 'Magic Mountain' for a striking viewpoint over the turquoise lagoon on the west side. Access is via a short but steep track.
Temae Beach, in the northeast near the airport, is one of French Polynesia's most beautiful. Immaculate white sand, transparent waters, and direct views of Tahiti in the distance.
The Manutea fruit juice distillery-factory in Opunohu Bay processes pineapples and local fruits. Free tasting of juices, liqueurs, and artisanal jams.
Want a cultural show? Polynesian evenings offered on the island combine ma'a tahiti buffets, traditional dances, percussion, and fire shows under the stars.

activities.text: Three Coconuts Pass hike from Opunohu Lookout. The trail crosses dense tropical forest and emerges at a pass with views over both bays and the lagoon on the Haapiti side.
Polynesian cooking class: preparing poisson cru with lime and coconut milk, the emblematic dish of the fenua. Some guesthouses offer this workshop.

visits.text: The village of Fare, on Huahine Nui's west coast, is the arrival point on the island. A few streets lined with craft shops, a small harbor, and a white sand beach facing the lagoon—the atmosphere is that of a Polynesian village frozen in time. No traffic lights, no luxury resorts: Huahine cultivates its wild side.

Take the road to Maeva village in the island's northeast. It's the largest archaeological site in French Polynesia: about forty marae (stone temples) stretch between Matairea hill and the shores of Lake Fauna Nui. Panels explain the function of each structure—some served for ceremonies, others for the investiture of chiefs.

At the edge of Lake Fauna Nui, you'll see the stone fish traps, restored and still used by locals. These basalt enclosures date back several centuries—fish enter with the rising tide and are trapped at low tide. An ingenious technique that still works today.

The community museum of Fare Pote'e, a large traditional house rebuilt on stilts at the lake's edge, displays archaeological objects found at the Maeva site: mother-of-pearl fishhooks, basalt adzes, and bone ornaments.

activities.text: Outrigger canoe tour on Huahine Nui's lagoon. The water is deep blue on the pass side and translucent turquoise on the motu side. You'll likely encounter rays and green turtles—Huahine is one of the islands where they're most present.
Late afternoon, settle on Fare beach for sunset. Fishing canoes return to port, children play in the warm water—a quiet moment, far from Bora Bora's tourist bustle.

visits.text: Huahine is actually two islands—Huahine Nui to the north and Huahine Iti to the south—linked by a bridge at Maroe. Cross this bridge and head south: vegetation thickens, dwellings become scarce, the road follows an increasingly wild lagoon.

Avea Bay, on Huahine Iti's southwest coast, is one of Polynesia's most beautiful anchorages. White sand, coconut palms leaning over the water, and a shallow lagoon that shifts from emerald green to cobalt blue. Few people—this is the anti-Bora Bora.

Marae Anini, at Huahine Iti's southern tip, extends on a rocky promontory facing the ocean. This basalt stone temple served for navigation-related ceremonies—logical for an island from which great canoes departed for the Leeward Islands.

Return via the east coast through Parea village. Gardens overflow with breadfruit, papayas, and banana trees. If you meet a local, chances are good they'll offer you a grapefruit—hospitality in Huahine isn't a myth.

activities.text: Snorkeling in Avea Bay's lagoon: the water is calm, shallow, and the corals are in good condition. Parrotfish, damselfish, and sometimes green turtles grazing the seagrass beds. Mask and snorkel are enough.

practical_info.tip: Sun protection essential—the light bottom reflects UV rays

activities.text: Island tour by bike or scooter. Huahine is about 30 km around—count on half a day by bike with stops. The road is flat, shaded, and you'll cross sleeping villages, coconut groves, and viewpoints over the lagoon.

visits.text: Take the east coast road, less frequented than the west. After about ten kilometers, Faie village is home to a stream where blue-eyed eels live, considered sacred by locals. They sometimes measure over a meter and will eat from your hand—a strange and slightly intimidating spectacle.

Continue to Bourayne Bay, nestled between two hills covered in dense vegetation. No beach here, but a viewpoint above that overlooks the entire bay and the lagoon beyond. It's the kind of place where you sit for ten minutes and stay an hour.

Return via the interior coast through the plantations. Huahine cultivates vanilla, noni, watermelon, and melon in open fields—roadside stalls sell the morning's fruits. Stop, chat, buy a bunch of bananas for next to nothing.

activities.text: This is a day to breathe. No tight schedule—settle on Fare beach with a book, swim when the mood strikes, watch fishing canoes return in early afternoon. Huahine is meant to be savored slowly, and that's precisely why you come.

Late afternoon, walk to Fare wharf for sunset. Locals gather there, some fishing from the dock, children jumping in the water. If a local offers you a taste of poisson cru they've just prepared, don't refuse.

map_keywords: Passe sacrée Te Ava Mo'a Raiatea

visits.text: Uturoa, Raiatea's main town, stretches along a quiet waterfront. Its small covered market sells fish, tropical fruits, and local crafts. The atmosphere is much calmer than Papeete.

Marae Taputapuātea, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2017, extends along the lagoon at Raiatea's southeastern point. Dedicated to the god 'Oro, this political, ceremonial, and funerary center brought together chiefs from across the Polynesian triangle. Several marae with distinct functions line a vast site of basalt stones facing the sacred Te Ava Mo'a pass.
Facing the marae, the sacred mountain Tea'etapu dominates the Opoa valley. A trail leads to the summit for a plunging view over the site, lagoon, and ocean.

Raiatea is the cradle of Ma'ohi civilization: it's from this island that ancient Polynesian navigators set out to colonize Hawaii, New Zealand, and Easter Island. The Taputapuātea site remains a pilgrimage destination for Polynesians worldwide.

activities.text: Boat or kayak ascent of the Faaroa River, French Polynesia's only navigable river. The motorized canoe ventures into a lush jungle of tree ferns, purau, and mape (Polynesian chestnuts). The guide recounts legends of the valley.
Swimming and snorkeling at Motu Iriru or Ofetaro, two public islets in Raiatea's lagoon accessible by boat. White sand, crystal water, and tropical fish.

visits.text: Taha'a, nicknamed the Vanilla Island, shares the same lagoon as Raiatea—30 minutes by boat is enough to reach it. The island produces about 80% of all French Polynesia's vanilla, the Vanilla tahitensis variety prized by chefs worldwide.

Visit to a vanilla plantation: cultivators explain the manual pollination of flowers, the harvest, and the long months of preparation needed to obtain the brown, fragrant pods. The sweet aroma permeates the greenhouse.

Stop at a family pearl farm to understand the grafting of Pinctada margaritifera oysters. Live demonstration and explanations on the classification of Tahitian black pearls—luster, orient, shape, and size.
Taha'a's rum distillery produces organic agricultural rum from sugarcane grown on former vanilla plots. Tasting available on-site.

activities.text: Drift snorkeling in the coral garden of Motu Tau Tau, northwest of Taha'a. You let yourself be carried by the current above a natural aquarium of colorful corals, anemones, and tropical fish. Shallow, accessible to all.

practical_info.tip: Sun protection essential—the light bottom reflects UV rays

activities.text: Lunch on a deserted motu: Polynesian barbecue of grilled fish, cooked bananas, and coconut milk, feet in the white sand facing the turquoise lagoon.
Watching blacktip reef sharks in the shallows on the reef side. They swim in very shallow water and pose no danger—still, maintain a reasonable distance.

visits.text: The plane circles Bora Bora's lagoon before landing on Motu Mute—the old airstrip built by the Americans in 1942. A boat shuttle connects to Vaitape, the island's main town.

Matira Beach, at the southern tip of the main island: a kilometer and a half of white sand edged with coconut palms. It's Bora Bora's only public beach, and one of Polynesia's most photographed. The water is shallow, the bottom sandy—ideal for swimming.
From Matira, Mount Otemanu (727 m) dominates the landscape. Its basalt summit is Bora Bora's symbol—impossible to miss, it's silhouetted against the sky from anywhere in the lagoon.
The small village of Vaitape stretches along the wharf. A few craft and black pearl shops line the main road. The atmosphere is calm, far from the bustle of resorts on the motus.

activities.text: Lagoon boat tour: coral gardens, swimming with fearless stingrays, and watching blacktip reef sharks in the shallows between motus. Respect distances with wildlife.
Sunset from Matira Beach: the sky turns orange and pink while Mount Otemanu stands out in silhouette. Bring a picnic.

visits.text: Bora Bora still bears the traces of Operation Bobcat. In 1942, 4,000 GIs landed on this island of 1,000 inhabitants to make it a supply base in the Pacific. Eight 7-inch guns and anti-aircraft batteries were installed on the heights.

The Anau guns at Pointe Fitiu are the island's best preserved. Double battery on reinforced concrete base, fort, ammunition shelter, and trench network—all restored and explained by trilingual panels installed in 2021.
Continue the island tour through Faanui Bay and its church at the mountain's foot. Two more guns are at Pointe Tereia, accessible by a short trail with views over the motus and overwater bungalows.
The Popoti lookout, a former American radar post on the west coast, offers the best panoramic view of Bora Bora. Access via a steep uphill 4x4 track—on foot or by quad.
At Vaitape wharf, a commemorative plaque recalls the Seabees' presence (Navy construction battalions). At Motu Mute airport, a chronological frieze traces the history of Operation Bobcat.

activities.text: Island tour by bike or scooter: the circular road is about 32 km on flat terrain. You pass luxury hotels, fishing villages, and viewpoints over the lagoon.

practical_info.duration: ~2 hrs by bike, ~1 hr by scooter

activities.text: Outrigger canoe (va'a) outing at sunset. You glide across the lagoon while Otemanu glows red—book for golden hour.

visits.text: Day devoted to Bora Bora's east lagoon, less frequented than the Matira side. The Anau site is known for its manta rays—these silent giants of 3 to 4 meters wingspan come to be cleaned by small fish at a natural cleaning station.

Motu Tapu, a small islet in the lagoon's south, served as the setting for a 1929 silent film—Tabu, directed by Murnau. It's a sandbank bordered by a few coconut palms, in the middle of unreal water. You can't land (it's a reserve), but you admire it from the boat.

If you haven't yet done a complete lagoon tour, today's the day. The boat passes over coral gardens between motus, stops for lunch on a deserted islet, and runs along the coral barrier on the ocean side.

activities.text: Scuba diving at the Anau site to observe manta rays. Even without certification, supervised discovery dives are offered—you descend to 10-12 meters with an instructor and the rays pass above you.
Late afternoon free on Matira Beach—make the most of it, it's your last day in Bora Bora. The lagoon is especially beautiful late in the day when the low light brings out the shades of blue.

visits.text: Forget everything you've seen since the start of the trip. In an hour's flight from Bora Bora, the landscape shifts: volcanic peaks disappear, the horizon flattens, and suddenly there's nothing but water—blue in every direction. Then a line of sand and coconut palms appears, barely higher than the waves. Welcome to an atoll. Rangiroa is the world's second largest: its lagoon is 80 km long and 30 wide. From the shore, you can't see the other side.

Avatoru village stretches along a strip of land so narrow you can see the lagoon on one side and the ocean on the other. A few colorful houses, a coral church, dogs asleep on the road—the rhythm of life here follows the tides.

Walk to Tiputa Pass, east of the village. It's a 200-meter-wide channel where the lagoon current meets the ocean. From the edge, you can watch spinner dolphins surfing the current in late afternoon—they come by the dozens.
The small Tiputa lighthouse, at the pass entrance, offers a viewpoint over the dolphin ballet and the current's changing colors—sometimes deep blue, sometimes emerald green depending on the tide.

activities.text: Drift snorkeling in Tiputa Pass (with a guide). The current carries you through the channel among Napoleon wrasses, jacks, barracudas, and sometimes grey reef sharks. It's impressive but supervised—the guide chooses the timing based on the current.
Dinner of grilled fish at a local guesthouse. Atolls live from fishing—the fish is ultra-fresh, prepared simply with lime and coconut milk. Try the marinated raw yellowfin tuna, a Tuamotu specialty.

visits.text: Full-day excursion to the Blue Lagoon, an hour by boat southwest of Avatoru. It's a lagoon within the lagoon—a natural basin bounded by a chain of motus where the water is an almost unreal pale blue. The white sand bottom is at 1 meter depth for hundreds of meters.

Île aux Récifs, south of the atoll, is a lunar landscape: fossilized coral formations sculpted by erosion form mushrooms, arches, and labyrinths of grey stone in the middle of the lagoon. It looks like a garden of natural sculptures.
Stop at a deserted motu with pink sand beaches—the color comes from red coral fragments mixed with white sand. The place is completely wild, without any construction.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling in the Blue Lagoon: juvenile blacktip reef sharks swim in the shallow water between your legs. They measure 40-60 cm and are harmless—children love it. Mask and snorkel provided by the operator.

practical_info.tip: Bring sunscreen and water—no shade on the motus

activities.text: Barbecue lunch on a motu: the guide prepares grilled fish over a coconut wood fire, accompanied by coconut milk rice and tropical fruits. You eat with your feet in the sand, alone in the middle of the Pacific.

visits.text: Rangiroa is home to a vineyard—yes, wine on a Pacific atoll. The estate produces white and rosé wine from grape varieties adapted to the tropical climate. The vines grow on coral sand and yield two harvests per year. The visit is short but surprising.

Visit to a pearl farm on the atoll. The grafter shows you how they insert a nucleus into the Pinctada margaritifera oyster, then explains the 18-month wait before harvest. Tuamotu mother-of-pearl produces pearls with green, aubergine, and peacock reflections—different from Society Islands pearls.

Walk along the reef on the ocean side, between Avatoru and Tiputa. The road crosses coconut groves and runs along deserted beaches beaten by the swell. The contrast between the lagoon's calm and the ocean's power is striking.

activities.text: Scuba diving at Avatoru Pass—Rangiroa's second major spot. You'll encounter schools of jacks, grey sharks, Napoleon wrasses, and sometimes hammerhead sharks between December and March. Even for experienced divers, it's a highlight.

Last evening on the atoll. The Tuamotu sky, without light pollution, is one of Polynesia's best for stargazing. The Milky Way is visible to the naked eye—a spectacle you can't see anywhere in Europe anymore.

visits.text: Fakarava is an atoll classified as a UNESCO biosphere reserve—one of the few in the world. Sixty kilometers long and 25 wide, it's pierced by two passes that rank among the planet's best dive sites.

Rotoava village, in the atoll's northeast, is Fakarava's only real village. A few hundred inhabitants, a white coral church, houses on stilts, and a fishing wharf. The church, built entirely of coral blocks by missionaries, is worth a stop for its unique architecture.

The north pass, Garuae, is Polynesia's widest—1,600 meters of opening. From the shore, you see the current tracing turquoise lines in the darker water. It's through this pass that sharks, rays, dolphins, and schools of tuna transit.
Walk along the lagoon shore, south of Rotoava. The sand is white, scattered with shells and polished coral pieces. Not a sound, not a building—just the lagoon, coconut palms, and wind.

activities.text: Snorkeling at the entrance to Garuae Pass, on the lagoon side. The incoming current brings crystal water and schools of tropical fish. Stay in the calm zone and let the fish come to you—the density of underwater life is stunning.

practical_info.tip: Snorkel at rising tide when current enters the lagoon

activities.text: Sunset from Rotoava beach. With no relief on the horizon, the sun plunges directly into the Pacific—a perfect orange disk that disappears in seconds.

visits.text: Full-day excursion to the south pass, Tumakohua, at the atoll's opposite end. The boat journey takes about 1.5 hours and crosses the lagoon in its full length—an expanse of turquoise water as far as the eye can see.

The abandoned village of Tetamanu, beside the south pass, was once the atoll's capital. A coral church built in 1874 remains—tiny and moving, with rough wooden benches and an altar facing the sea. A few isolated guesthouses have brought life back around the village.

The south pass, narrower than Garuae (200 meters), concentrates marine life. This is where the famous 'shark wall' forms—hundreds of grey reef sharks that gather in the pass current, especially at rising tide.

activities.text: Diving in Tumakohua Pass—THE Fakarava spot. You descend into the current amid 200 to 700 grey sharks. They're not interested in you—they're hunting in the current. It's a unique spectacle, regularly ranked among the world's top 10 dives.
Lunch on the motu near Tetamanu, between two dives or while waiting for divers. Poisson cru, coconut rice, fruits—the meal is simple and served under a fare (palm shelter) with your feet in the sand.

visits.text: Last morning on the atoll. Borrow a bike and ride along the Rotoava road—it runs just a few kilometers along the lagoon. You'll pass coconut groves where locals dry copra, coconut flesh cut and laid out in the sun. It's been the atoll's main economic resource for over a century, long before tourism.

At low tide, go down to the coral platform on the east side. You walk on an exposed reef dotted with pools where miniature worlds thrive—pencil urchins, giant clams with electric blue lips, sea stars, small trapped fish waiting for the tide. Take time to look: each pool is an aquarium.

Make a last stop at the northern point, where the reef meets the entrance to Garuae Pass. The current traces veins of color in the water—from jade green to midnight blue depending on depth and tide. It's the kind of view you watch for a long time, trying to memorize it.

activities.text: Last dive or snorkel at Garuae for those who want more. Otherwise, spend the morning in the lagoon water by the village—knee-deep, sandy bottom, colorful corals within mask's reach. No boat or guide needed.

The Tuamotus are one of the last places on Earth without light pollution. If the sky is clear tonight, go out after dinner: the Milky Way forms a complete arc from horizon to horizon, so dense it casts a shadow. It may be the most striking memory of the entire journey.

visits.text: Back in Tahiti for the final two days, head toward the peninsula (Tahiti Iti), often ignored by visitors. The Taravao isthmus connects the two parts of the island—beyond it, the road narrows, vegetation thickens, tourists disappear.
Marae Arahurahu in Paea (PK 22.5 west coast) is Tahiti's best restored. Its terraced stone platform, surrounded by coconut palms, still hosts reenactments of traditional ceremonies.
The Maraa Caves, carved in the coastal cliff at Paea, shelter pools of crystal freshwater in a fern setting. A quick and surprising stop.

Climb to the Taravao plateau, at 600 meters altitude. The road winds through pastures (yes, cows in Tahiti) and opens onto a panorama embracing the entire north coast, lagoon, and Moorea in the distance. On clear days, it's one of the island's most beautiful viewpoints.

Descend toward the south coast and Teahupo'o village, famous in the surfing world for its mythical wave. From shore, you don't see much—the wave forms on the reef, 500 meters offshore. But the village itself, wedged between mountain and lagoon, is worth the detour for its atmosphere.

Continue to the road's end on the east side, toward Tautira village. The road stops there—beyond is the Pari cliff, accessible only on foot or by boat. The village is quiet, bordered by a river and a protected lagoon.

activities.text: Swimming in Teahupo'o's lagoon, on the inside (not the wave side). The water is calm, warm, and the bottom sandy. Some operators offer boat trips to see the wave up close—even without surfing, the spectacle of the swell rising on the reef is impressive.

practical_info.tip: The wave is only active May to October with southern swells

activities.text: Lunch at a local peninsula snack bar: poisson cru, ma'a tinito (Sino-Tahitian dish), or steak-frites—everyday Tahitian cooking, far from tourist restaurants.

map_keywords: Tombeau Roi Pomare V Arue Tahiti

visits.text: Last morning in Papeete. Return to the market—not to buy this time, but to observe. The first day you were a tourist; twenty days later you recognize the flowers, fruits, smells. The tiare crown seller greets you because she's seen you pass three times. That's the measure of a journey: the gaze has changed.

King Pomare V's tomb in Arue on the east coast houses the burial place of Tahiti's last king, who died in 1891. The white coral monument topped with a funerary urn overlooks the lagoon—a quiet and somewhat melancholic place, like a kingdom that faded without noise.

If your flight is in the evening (many international flights depart at night), save the afternoon for the beach at PK 18 in Punaauia. White sand, calm water, Moorea opposite—it's your last image of Polynesia, might as well make it beautiful.

activities.text: Last-minute shopping at the market or downtown shops: vanilla pods, handmade monoï, hand-painted pareos. Fresh tiare crowns won't survive the journey, but their fragrance on the plane is worth the gesture.
Transfer to Faa'a airport, 5 km from Papeete. Arrive with margin—check-in for international flights opens 3 hours before departure. The last Polynesian sunset is visible from the tarmac.

seo.h1_title: Complete Polynesia: Society & Tuamotu Islands in 20 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: This 20-day itinerary crosses two French Polynesian archipelagos. First part in the Society Islands: markets and waterfalls in Tahiti, volcanic ridges in Moorea, preserved authenticity in Huahine, UNESCO heritage in Raiatea, legendary lagoon in Bora Bora. Then head to the Tuamotus—Rangiroa and Fakarava—where shark passes and deserted atolls offer a completely different face of Polynesia.
seo.seo_hook: Two archipelagos, eight islands, 20 days: from the Society volcanoes to the Tuamotu coral atolls, the most complete Polynesian circuit
featured_list: Tahiti: Papeete market, Faarumai waterfalls, and wild west coast
Huahine: 40 marae at Maeva, ancestral fish traps, and pristine lagoon
Raiatea: Marae Taputapuātea, UNESCO site and cradle of Polynesian civilization
Fakarava: Tumakohua Pass and its wall of 700 sharks, UNESCO biosphere reserve

places_taglines.PF::papeete: Where the market's dawn chorus meets harbor-side food trucks under Moorea's watchful gaze
places_taglines.PF::paopao: Between two bays pierced by volcanic spires, life slows to the rhythm of outrigger paddles
places_taglines.PF::fare: An island where forty stone temples whisper of ancient chiefs and the sea still remembers their names
places_taglines.PF::uturoa: Gateway to the sacred island where Polynesian navigators once set sail to conquer the Pacific
places_taglines.PF::vaitape: A quiet harbor town cradled by the South Pacific's most impossibly blue lagoon
places_taglines.PF::avatoru: Where spinner dolphins ride the pass at sunset and the lagoon stretches beyond sight
places_taglines.PF::rotoava: Coral churches and UNESCO waters where seven hundred sharks hunt in liquid sapphire

seo_keywords: tahiti moorea bora bora rangiroa fakarava

highlights: Tahiti → Moorea ferry: 30-minute crossing with views of the twin bays
Bora Bora → Rangiroa flight: transition from volcanic peaks to coral atolls
Tumakohua Pass at Fakarava: diving among 200 to 700 grey reef sharks
Marae Taputapuātea: sacred UNESCO site from which great Polynesian navigators departed
